source.title: Oncologic Emergencies: Recognition and Initial Management

signals: requires inpatient therapy with rapid administration

extraction.summary: Oncologic emergencies can be classified as metabolic, hematologic, structural, or treatment-related conditions requiring urgent recognition and management. Early stabilization and subspecialty referral are often necessary to prevent devastating outcomes including paralysis and death.

population.risk_criteria: Cancer patients, particularly those receiving chemotherapy, radiation therapy, or immunotherapy
population.disease_definition: Acute life-threatening complications in cancer patients categorized as metabolic, hematologic, structural, or treatment-related
population.severity_staging: Ranges from insidious development over months to acute presentations over hours

recommendations.recommendation: Emergent use of antibiotics in febrile neutropenia (within 30 minutes of presentation)
Evaluate new-onset back pain in cancer patients as epidural spinal cord compression until ruled out
recommendations.notes: Treatment delay can lead to permanent paralysis

recommendations.recommendation: Use permanent surgical solutions for malignant pericardial effusions
recommendations.notes: Pericardial windows and indwelling catheters superior to pericardiocentesis alone

recommendations.recommendation: Vigorous rehydration and maintain urine output for tumor lysis syndrome
recommendations.notes: Focus on stabilization with uric acid reduction

recommendations.recommendation: Concurrent palliative care consultation at time of cancer diagnosis
Concurrent palliative care consultation should be offered to patients with cancer at the time of diagnosis
Emergent use of antibiotics in patients with cancer who present with febrile neutropenia improves survival rates
recommendations.notes: Time to antibiotic administration impacts mortality

recommendations.recommendation: New-onset back pain in patients with cancer should be evaluated as epidural spinal cord compression until it is ruled out

More permanent surgical solutions for management of malignant pericardial effusions, such as pericardial windows and indwelling pericardial catheters, are associated with improved patient outcomes compared with percutaneous pericardiocentesis alone
recommendations.notes: Better long-term outcomes than pericardiocentesis alone

medications.indication: Hypercalcemia of malignancy - diuresis after rehydration
Malignant epidural spinal cord compression, superior vena cava syndrome

red_flags: New-onset back pain in cancer patients, especially worse when lying down
Facial edema with collateral venous circulation
Rapid neurologic decline or motor deficits
Beck triad: muffled heart sounds, hypotension, increased JVP
Progressive decline in mental function with hypercalcemia
Severe electrolyte abnormalities after cancer treatment
New-onset back pain in cancer patients (possible spinal cord compression)
Beck triad: muffled heart sounds, hypotension, increased jugular venous pressure
extraction.follow_up: Inpatient monitoring until stabilization achieved, with specific parameters: afebrile 48-72 hours and ANC >500 cells/mm³ for febrile neutropenia; euvolemia achievement for hypercalcemia; correction of electrolyte abnormalities for tumor lysis syndrome
extraction.escalation_path: Immediate oncology consultation for all emergencies; nephrology for tumor lysis syndrome and severe renal insufficiency requiring hemodialysis; neurosurgery for epidural spinal cord compression; cardiothoracic surgery for superior vena cava syndrome and pericardial effusions; hematology for hyperviscosity syndrome; infectious disease for febrile neutropenia; palliative care consultation recommended for advanced disease
